text:
Advantages and benefits of cloud computing:

Trade capital expense for {{c1::variable}} expense.

Advantages and benefits of cloud computing:

Benefit from massive {{c1::economies of scales.}}

Advantages and benefits of cloud computing:

Stop guessing {{c1::capacity.}}

Advantages and benefits of cloud computing:

Increase ___ and ___

{{c1::speed, agility}}

Advantages and benefits of cloud computing:

Stop spending money on running and maintaining {{c1::data centers}}

Advantages and benefits of cloud computing:

Go {{c1::global}} in minutes.
SaaS stands for {{c1::Software as a Service}}
PaaS stands for {{c1::Platform as a Service}}
IaaS stands for {{c1::Infrastructure as a Service}}
The consumer for a SaaS is a {{c1::customer.}}
The consumer for a PaaS is a {{c1::developer.}}
The consumer for a IaaS is an {{c1::admin.}}

There are three models for cloud computing:

{{c1::Cloud, Hybrid, and On-Premise}}

AWS Global Infrastructure:

{{c1::Regions}} are physical locations in the world with multiple availability zones

AWS Global Infrastructure:

{{c1::Availability zones}} are one or more discrete data centers.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

An {{c1::edge location}} is a datacenter owned by a trusted partner of AWS.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

A region is a {{c1::geographically}} distinct location which has multiple AZ's.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

Every region is physically isolated from each other in terms of three things:

{{c1::location, water, and power}}

AWS Global Infrastructure:

Each region has at least {{c1::two}} AZ's

AWS Global Infrastructure:

The largest region is {{c1::US-EAST}}

AWS Global Infrastructure:

New services almost always become available in the {{c1::US-East}} region first.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

Not all services are available in all {{c1::regions.}}

AWS Global Infrastructure:

{{c1::US-East-1}} is the region where you see your billing information.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

An {{c1::AZ}} is a datacenter owned and operated by AWS which AWS services run.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

{{c1::AZ's}} are represented by a Region Code, followed by a letter identifier e.g. us-east-1a

AWS Global Infrastructure:

{{c1::Multi-AZ}} configuration allows distributing your instances allows failover configuration for handling requests when one goes down.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

< {{c1::10ms}} latency between AZ's

AWS Global Infrastructure:

An {{c1::Edge location}} is a datacenter owned by a trusted partner of AWS with a direct connection to the AWS network.

AWS Global Infrastructure:

These locations serve requests for ___ and ___. Requests to either of these services will be routed to the nearest edge location automatically.

{{c1::Cloudfront, Route 53.}}

AWS Global Infrastructure:

Edge locations allow for low {{c1::latency}} no matter where the end user is located.
{{c1::GovCloud}} Regions allow customers to host sensitive controlled unclassified information and other types of regulated workloads.
{{c1::GovCloud}} Regions are only operated by employees who are citizens of the U.S., on U.S. soil.
GovCloud regions are only accessible to U.S. entities and root account holders who pass a {{c1::screening process.}}
IAM stands for {{c1::Identity and Access Management}}
The advantage of using the sessions manager over ssh access to the server is that sessions manager logs {{c1::everytime someone uses a session.}}
An {{c1::Amazon Machine Image}} (AMI) provides the information required to launch an instance.
{{c1::Auto scaling groups}} (ASG) are used to meet the demand of whatever traffic you have.
ELB stands for {{c1::Elastic Load Balancer.}}
{{c1::Elastic Load Balancer}} evenly distributes load between multiple instances.
{{c1::S3}} is used for storing files.
CDN stands for {{c1::content distribution network.}}
Cloudfront is an example of a {{c1::CDN.}}
{{c1::Relational Database Service}} allows you to create databases hosted by AWS.
When you launch an EC2 instance, by default the pricing is {{c1::on-demand.}}
EC2 stands for {{c1::elastic compute cloud}}
In on-demand pricing you are charged by the {{c1::hour or minute.}}
On-demand pricing is best for cases where the workload is {{c1::short term, spikey, or unpredictable.}}
{{c1::Reserved instances}} pricing is best for long-term, steady state, predictable usage applications.

Pricing:

{{c1::Reserved instances}} can be shared between multiple accounts in an organization.

Pricing:

Unused RI's can be sold on the {{c1::Reserved Instance Marketplace.}}

Pricing:

Reserved instances allow for three payment models:

{{c1::
All upfront
Partial upfront
No upfront
}}

Pricing:

{{c1::Spot instances}} allow Amazon to utilize unused capacity on their servers by offering the capacity at a discount, like when a hotel offers cut rates on rooms to fill up the hotel.

Pricing:

Spot Instances can offer discounts as much as {{c1::90%}} compared to on-demand.

Pricing:

{{c1::Spot instances}} work for applications which can have flexibile start or end times, or are only feasible at very low compute costs.

Pricing:

{{c1::AWS Batch}} is an easy way to use Spot Pricing.

Pricing:

The most expensive option for instances is {{c1::Dedicated Host.}}

Pricing:

{{c1::Dedicated hosting}} allows customers to each have their own physical machine, not share a machine with other users.

Pricing:

Dedicated Host is offered in two flavors:

{{c1::
on-demand
reserved
}}

Pricing:

Reserved instances come in three classes:

{{c1::standard
convertable - can be upgraded
schedulable - can run at certain times}}

Support:

AWS has 4 support plans:

{{c1::Basic
Developer
Business 
Enterprise}}

Support:

Basic support costs {{c1::$0}} per month

Support:

Developer support costs {{c1::$20}} per month.

Support:

Business support costs {{c1::$100}} per month.

Support

Enterprise support costs {{c1::$15,000}} per month.

Support:

Which level has individuals assigned to help you with your account?

{{c1::Enterprise}}

Support:

Which levels offer support for 3rd party applications?

{{c1::Business and enterprise}}

Support:

Which levels offer technical support via email?

{{c1::Dev, Business and Enterprise}}

Support:

Which level offers the fastest response time?

{{c1::Enterprise}}

Support:

Basic and developer levels offer {{c1::7}} advisor checks.

Support:

Business and enterprise offer {{c1::all}} advisor checks.

Pricing:

RI's require you to commit to a ___ or ___ year commitment.

{{c1::1, 3}}
{{c1::AWS Marketplace}} is a curated digitial catalog of thousands of listings from independent software vendors.
{{c1::AWS Automated Advisor}} advises you on security, saving money, performance, service limits, and fault tolerance.

There are 5 categories of checks for AWS Trusted Advisor:

{{c1::Cost Optimization
Performance
Security
Fault Tolerance
Service Limits
}}
{{c1::Consolidated billing}} is a feature which give you one bill for all of your accounts.
Use {{c1::cost explorer}} to visualize usage for consolidated billing.
AWS has {{c1::Volume Discounts}} for many services, so the more you use, the more you save.
{{c1::AWS Cost Explorer}} lets you visualize, understand, and manage your AWS costs and usage over time.
{{c1::AWS budgets}} give the you ability to setup alerts if you exceed or are approaching your defined budget.
TCO calculator stands for {{c1::Total Cost of Ownership calculator.}}
{{c1::Total Cost of Ownership}} provides you with a detailed set of pricing reports which can be used in executive presentations.
{{c1::AWS Landing Zone}} helps enterprises quickly setup a secure AWS multi-account.
AWS Landing Zone provides you with a baseline to get started with a {{c1::multi-account architecture.}}
{{c1::AWS Account Vending Machine}} automatically provisions and configures new accounts via Service Catalog Template.
Account Vending Machine uses {{c1::Single Sign-On}} (SSO) to manage and access accounts.
{{c1::Tags}} are words or phrases that act as metadata for organizing your AWS resources.
{{c1::Resource groups}} are a collection of resources that share one or more tags.
{{c1::AWS Quick Starts}} are prebuilt templates by AWS and AWS partners to help you deploy popular stacks on AWS
Most Quick Start reference deployments all you to spin up a fully functional architecture in less than {{c1::an hour.}}
{{c1::AWS Cost and Usage Report}} generates spreadsheets, enabling you to analyze and understand your AWS costs.
Cost and Usage Reports places your report in {{c1::S3.}}
Amazon S3 stands for {{c1::Simple Storage Service.}}
With Cost and Usage Reports, you can use {{c1::Athena}} to turn the report into a queryable database.
With Cost and Usage Reports, you can use {{c1::Quicksight}} to visualize your billing data as graphs.

Organizations and accounts:

{{c1::Root Account User}} is a single sign-on identity that has access to all AWS services and resources in an account.

Organizations and accounts:

{{c1::Organizations}} allow you to centrally manage billing, central control, compliance, security, and resources across your AWS accounts.

Organizations and accounts:

_____ are a group of AWS accounts within an organization which can also contain other _____, creating a hierarchy. 

{{c1::organization units}}
{{c1::Service control policies}} give central access over the allowed permissions for all accounts in your organization, helping to ensure your accounts stay within your organization's guidelines.
VPC stands for {{c1::Virtual Private Cloud}}

AWS Networking:

{{c1::Route tables}} determines where network traffic from your subnets are directed.

AWS Networking:

{{c1::Security groups}} act as firewalls at the instance level.

AWS Networking:

{{c1::Subnets}} are logical partitions of an IP network in smaller, multiple network units.

AWS Networking:

{{c1::Internet gateway}} enables access to the internet.

AWS Networking:

A {{c1::network access control list}} (NACL) is an optional layer of security for your VPC that acts as a firewall for controlling traffic in and out of one or more subnets.

AWS Database Services:

{{c1::DynamoDB}} is a NoSQL key/value database.

AWS Database:

{{c1::DocumentDB}} is a NoSQL Document database that is MongoDB compatible.

AWS Database:

{{c1::RDS}} is a service that supports multiple database engines.

AWS Database:

{{c1::Aurora}} is a fully managed MySQL and PSQL database service.

AWS Database:

{{c1::Aurora serverless}} is a database that only runs when you need it, like Lambda.

AWS Database:

{{c1::Neptune}} is a managed Graph database.

AWS Database:

{{c1::Redshift}} is a columnar, petabyte warehouse.

AWS Database:

{{c1::Elasticache}} is a Redis or Memcached database.

Provisioning:

{{c1::Elastic beanstalk}} is a service for deploying and scaling web applications developed with Java, .NET, PHP, NodeJS, Ruby, Go, Python, and Docker

Provisioning:

{{c1::OpsWorks}} is a configuration management service that provides instances of Chef and Puppet.
{{c1::CloudFormation}} provides instances as code in the form of JSON or YAML.
{{c1::Provisioning}} is the the allocation or creation of resources and services to a customer.

Computing:

ECS stands for {{c1::Elastic Container Service}}.

Computing:

{{c1::Elastic Container Service}} is Docker as a service.

Computing:

{{c1::Fargate}} is microservices where you don't have to think about infrastructure. Play per task.

Computing:

{{c1::Elastic Kubernetes Service}} is Kubernetes as a service. Easy to deploy, manage and scale containerized services using Kubernetes.

Computing:

{{c1::Lambda}} allows you to run code without provisioning or maintaining servers. Pay for only compute time.

Computing:

{{c1::Elastic Beanstalk}} orchestrates various AWS services.

Computing:

{{c1::AWS batch}} plans, schedules, and executes your batch workloads across the full range of AWS services and features, such as EC2 and Spot Instances.

Storage:

{{c1::S3}} is an object store. Can be thought of as a hard drive in the cloud.
{{c1::S3 Glacier}} is low cost storage for archiving and long-term backup.

Storage:

{{c1::Storage Gateway}} is hybrid cloud storage for local caching.

Storage:

EBS stands for {{c1::Elastic Block Storage.}}

Storage:

{{c1::Elastic Block Storage}} is like a hard drive in the cloud that you attach to EC2 instances.

Storage:

{{c1::Elastic File Storage}} is file storage mountable to multiple EC2 instances as the same time.

Storage:

{{c1::Snowball}} allows you to physically migrate lots of data via a computer suitcase.

Storage:

A Snowball suitcase can store {{c1::50-80}} terabytes.

Storage:

Snowball Edge can store {{c1::100}} terabytes.

Storage:

{{c1::Snowmobile}} is a shipping container pulled by a semi-truck.

Storage:

A Snowmobile can store up to {{c1::100 petabytes.}}

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::Amazon connect}} is a cloud-based call center service you can setup in just a few clicks.

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::WorkSpaces}} provides Virtual Remote Desktops for Linux or Windows machines which can scale up to thousands of desktops.

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::WorkDocs}} is a content creation and collaboration service, comparable to Sharepoint.

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::Chime}} is a platform for online meetings and video conferences which elastically scales to meet demand.

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::WorkMail}} is managed business email, contacts, and calendar service with support for existing email applications via IMAP.

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::Pinpoint}} is a marketing campaign management service which you can use for sending targeted emails, SMS messages, push notifications, and text messages.

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::Simple Email Service}} is a cloud-based email sending service designed for email marketers and application developers to send marketing and notification emails.

Business Centric Services:

{{c1::Quicksight}} is a business intelligence service which allows you to connect multiple data sources and quickly visualize data in the form of graphs with little to no programming knowledge.

Enterprise Integration:

{{c1::Direct Connect}} gives you a gigabit network connection from your premises directly to AWS.

Enterprise Integration:

{{c1::VPN}} establishes a secure network connection to your AWS network.

Enterprise Integration:

{{c1::Storage Gateway}} is a hybrid storage solution that enables your on-premises applications to use AWS storage.

Enterprise Integration:

{{c1::Active Directory}} enables your directory-aware workloads and AWS resources to use managed Active directory in the AWS cloud.

Logging Services:

{{c1::CloudTrail}} logs all API calls between AWS services.

Logging Services:

CloudTrail helps answer the question "Who can we {{c1::blame}}?"

Logging Services:

{{c1::CloudWatch}} is a collection of multiple logging services.

CloudWatch:

{{c1::Logs}} gives you performance data about AWS services.

CloudWatch:

{{c1::Metrics}} represents a time-ordered set of data points - a variable to monitor.

CloudWatch:

{{c1::Events}} trigger an event based on a condition such as a snapshot of the server every hour.

CloudWatch:

{{c1::Alarms}} trigger alarms based on metrics.

CloudWatch:

{{c1::Dashboard}} creates visualizations based on metrics.
SWF stands for {{c1::Simple Workflow Service}}.

Shared Responsibility - Security:

Customers are responsible for two things:

{{c1::
data
configuration
}}

Shared Responsibility - Security:

Customers are responsible for security {{c1::in}} the cloud.

Shared Responsibility - Security:

AWS is responsible for security {{c1::of}} the cloud.

Shared Responsibility - Security:

Amazon is responsible for three things:

{{c1::
Hardware
Operation of Managed Services
Global Infrastructure
}}

AWS Compliance Programs:

Two examples of the compliance programs AWS complies with include {{c1::HIPAA and PCI DSS.}}
{{c1::AWS Artifact}} is a no-cost self service portal for on-demand access to AWS compliance reports.

Security:

{{c1::Hardening}} is the act of removing as many security risks as possible.
{{c1::AWS Inspector}} runs a security benchmark against specific EC2 instances.

WAF:

Web Application Firewall protects your web application from common {{c1::web exploits. }}

WAF:

Write you own set of rules to allow or deny traffic based on the contents of an {{c1::HTTP request.}}

WAF:

Use a ruleset for an AWS partner from the {{c1::AWS WAF Rules Marketplace. }}

WAF:

Can be attached to two services:

{{c1::CloudFront
Application Load Balancer}}
DDoS stands for {{c1::Distributed Denial of Service.}}
{{c1::AWS Shield}} is a managed DDoS protections service that safeguards services running on AWS.
All AWS customers benefit from AWS Shield {{c1::Standard}} at no extra cost.
Higher level DDoS protection is available under {{c1::Shield Advanced.}}

Shield:

Shield Advanced costs {{c1::$3000}} per year.
{{c1::Amazon Route 53}} is a highly available and scalable cloud Domain Name System (DNS) web service
{{c1::Amazon CloudFront}} is a fast content delivery network (CDN).
{{c1::Pentest}} is an authorized simulated attack on a computer system performed to evaluate the security of the system.

Pen Testing is allowed for all services on AWS.

True/False

{{c1::False}}
{{c1::Guard Duty}} is a threat detection service which continuously monitors for malicious, suspicious activity and unauthorized behaviour.

Guard Duty:

GD uses {{c1::machine learning}} to analyze AWS logs.

Guard Duty:

GD will alert you to findings, which you can automate an {{c1::incident reponse}} to via Cloud Events or other 3rd party services.
KMS stands for {{c1::Key Management Service.}}
{{c1::Key Management Service}} is a managed service which makes it easy for you to create and control encyption keys used to encrypt your data.
HSM stands for {{c1::Hardware Security Module.}}
Envelope encryption involves encrypting your data key with a {{c1::master key.}}
{{c1::Amazon Macie}} is a fully managed service that monitors S3 data access for anomalies and generates automated results when it detects risks of unauthorized access or data leaks.
Macie works by using {{c1::machine learning}} to analyze your CloudTrail logs.
{{c1::NACL's}} act as firewalls at the subnet level.
Security Groups implicitly deny {{c1::all access}}.
The example of using a NACL is to {{c1::block a specific IP address.}}
Security Groups can't block a specific IP address because they implicitly {{c1::deny all access.}}

AWS VPN comes in two flavors:

{{c1::
Site to site VPN
Client VPN
}}
AWS Site-to-Site VPN allows an {{c1::entire office}} to connect to the cloud.
AWS Client VPN allow a {{c1::single user}} to connect to the cloud.
{{c1::CloudSearch}} is a search engine you could add to your ecommerce site.
{{c1::AWS Elemental MediaConnect}} is a high-quality transport service for live video.

{{c1::AWS Elemental MediaConvert}} is a file-based video transcoding service with broadcast-grade features. It allows you to easily create video-on-demand (VOD) content for broadcast and multiscreen delivery at scale.
{{c1::Simple Notification Service}} is generally used for sending plain text emails.
{{c1::Simple Notification Service}} is used to send notifications to subscribers of topics.
Rather than using Classic Load Balancer, you should choose ALB or NLB because {{c1::they have more features. }}
Elastic Load Balancer can also be referred to as {{c1::Classic Load Balancer or CLB.}}
Layer 7 is also known as the {{c1::application layer.}}
Layer 4,  the transport layer, ensures {{c1::complete data transfers.}}
{{c1::Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR)}} is a fully-managed Docker container registry that makes it easy for developers to store, manage, and deploy Docker container images.

Amazon {{c1::Simple Workflow Service}} helps developers build, run, and scale background jobs that have parallel or sequential steps. You can think of it as a fully-managed state tracker and task coordinator in the Cloud.

Amazon {{c1::Elastic Map Reduce}} is the industry-leading cloud big data platform for processing vast amounts of data using open source tools such as Apache Spark, Apache Hive, Apache HBase, Apache Flink, Apache Hudi, and Presto.
{{c1::Simple Systems Manager}} provides a unified user interface so you can view operational data from multiple AWS services and allows you to automate operational tasks across your AWS resources.
A {{c1::Hardware Security Module}} is designed to store keys securely in memory, so they are never written to disc.

AWS allows users to manage their resources using a web based user interface. What is the name of this interface?

{{c1::AWS Management Console.}}

Which of the following is an example of horizontal scaling in the AWS Cloud?

{{c1::Adding more EC2 instances of the same size to handle an increase in traffic.}}

question: You have noticed that several critical Amazon EC2 instances have been terminated. Which of the following AWS services would help you determine who took this action?
explanation: B. AWS CloudTrail.

question: Which of the below options are related to the reliability of AWS? (Choose TWO)

choices.text: Applying the principle of least privilege to all AWS resources.
Automatically provisioning new resources to meet demand.
All AWS services are considered Global Services, and this design helps customers serve their international users.
Providing compensation to customers if issues occur.
Ability to recover quickly from failures.

explanation:
B. Automatically provisioning new resources to meet demand.
E. Ability to recover quickly from failures.

text:
Which statement is true regarding the AWS Shared Responsibility Model?

{{c1::Responsibilities vary depending on the services used.}}

question: You have set up consolidated billing for several AWS accounts. One of the accounts has purchased a number of reserved instances for 3 years. Which of the following is true regarding this scenario?

choices.text: The Reserved Instance discounts can only be shared with the master account.
All accounts can receive the hourly cost benefit of the Reserved Instances.
The purchased instances will have better performance than On-demand instances.
There are no cost benefits from using consolidated billing; It is for informational purposes only.

explanation: B. All accounts can receive the hourly cost benefit of the Reserved Instances.

question: A company has developed an eCommerce web application in AWS. What should they do to ensure that the application has the highest level of availability?

choices.text: Deploy the application across multiple Availability Zones and Edge locations.
Deploy the application across multiple Availability Zones and subnets.
Deploy the application across multiple Regions and Availability Zones.
Deploy the application across multiple VPC’s and subnets.

explanation: C. Deploy the application across multiple Regions and Availability Zones.

question: What does AWS Snowball provide? (Choose TWO)

choices.text: Built-in computing capabilities that allow customers to process data locally.
A catalog of third-party software solutions that customers need to build solutions and run their businesses.
A hybrid cloud storage between on-premises environments and the AWS Cloud.
An Exabyte-scale data transfer service that allows you to move extremely large amounts of data to AWS.
Secure transfer of large amounts of data into and out of the AWS.

explanation:
A. Built-in computing capabilities that allow customers to process data locally.
E. Secure transfer of large amounts of data into and out of the AWS.

question: A company has an AWS Enterprise Support plan. They want quick and efficient guidance with their billing and account inquiries. Which of the following should the company use?
explanation: B. AWS Support Concierge.

question: A Japanese company hosts their applications on Amazon EC2 instances in the Tokyo Region. The company has opened new branches in the United States, and the US users are complaining of high latency. What can the company do to reduce latency for the users in the US while minimizing costs?

choices.text: Applying the Amazon Connect latency-based routing policy.
Registering a new US domain name to serve the users in the US.
Building a new data center in the US and implementing a hybrid model.
Deploying new Amazon EC2 instances in a Region located in the US.

explanation: D. Deploying new Amazon EC2 instances in a Region located in the US.

question: An organization has a large number of technical employees who operate their AWS Cloud infrastructure. What does AWS provide to help organize them into teams and then assign the appropriate permissions for each team?
explanation: C. IAM user groups.

question: A company has decided to migrate its Oracle database to AWS. Which AWS service can help achieve this without negatively impacting the functionality of the source database?
explanation: B. AWS Database Migration Service.

text:
Adjusting compute capacity dynamically to reduce cost is an implementation of which AWS cloud best practice?

{{c1::Implement elasticity.}}

question: What are the benefits of having infrastructure hosted in AWS? (Choose TWO)

choices.text: There is no need to worry about security.
Gaining complete control over the physical infrastructure.
Operating applications on behalf of customers.
All of the physical security and most of the data/network security are taken care of for you.

explanation:
A. Increasing speed and agility.
E. All of the physical security and most of the data/network security are taken care of for you.

text:
What is the advantage of the AWS-recommended practice of "decoupling" applications?

{{c1::Reduces inter-dependencies so that failures do not impact other components of the application.}}

question: Which of the following helps a customer view the Amazon EC2 billing activity for the past month?
explanation: D. AWS Cost & Usage Reports.

text:
What do you gain from setting up consolidated billing for five different AWS accounts under another master account?

{{c1::Each AWS account gets volume discounts.}}

question: What should you do in order to keep the data on EBS volumes safe? (Choose TWO)

choices.text: Regularly update firmware on EBS devices.
Ensure that EBS data is encrypted at rest.
Store a backup daily in an external drive.
Prevent any unauthorized access to AWS data centers.

explanation:
B. Create EBS snapshots.
C. Ensure that EBS data is encrypted at rest.

text:
One of the most important AWS best-practices to follow is the cloud architecture principle of elasticity. How does this principle improve your architecture’s design?

{{c1::By automatically provisioning the required AWS resources based on changes in demand.}}

question: A startup company is operating on limited funds and is extremely concerned about cost overruns. Which of the below options can be used to notify the company when their monthly AWS bill exceeds $2000? (Choose TWO)

choices.text: Setup a CloudWatch billing alarm that triggers an SNS notification when the threshold is exceeded.
Configure the Amazon Simple Email Service to send billing alerts to their email address on a daily basis.
Configure the AWS Budgets Service to alert the company when the threshold is exceeded.
Configure AWS CloudTrail to automatically delete all AWS resources when the threshold is exceeded.
Configure the Amazon Connect Service to alert the company when the threshold is exceeded.

explanation:
A. Setup a CloudWatch billing alarm that triggers an SNS notification when the threshold is exceeded.
C. Configure the AWS Budgets Service to alert the company when the threshold is exceeded.

text:
What does Amazon CloudFront use to distribute content to global users with low latency?

{{c1::AWS Edge Locations.}}

What does the "Principle of Least Privilege" refer to?

{{c1::You should grant your users only the permissions they need when they need them and nothing more.}}

Which of the following does NOT belong to the AWS Cloud Computing models?

{{c1::Networking as a Service (NaaS).}}

question: The identification process of an online financial services company requires that new users must complete an online interview with their security team. The completed recorded interviews are only required in the event of a legal issue or a regulatory compliance breach. What is the most cost-effective service to store the recorded videos?
explanation: C. Amazon S3 Glacier Deep Archive.

text:
Which service provides DNS in the AWS cloud?

{{c1::Route 53.}}

question: Hundreds of thousands of DDoS attacks are recorded every month worldwide. What service does AWS provide to help protect AWS Customers from these attacks? (Choose TWO)
explanation:
A. AWS Shield.
D. AWS WAF.

question: A company is deploying a new two-tier web application in AWS. Where should the most frequently accessed data be stored so that the application’s response time is optimal?
explanation: D. Amazon ElastiCache.

text:
You want to run a questionnaire application for only one day (without interruption), which Amazon EC2 purchase option should you use?

{{c1::On-demand instances.}}

question: You are working on a project that involves creating thumbnails of millions of images. Consistent uptime is not an issue, and continuous processing is not required. Which EC2 buying option would be the most cost-effective?
explanation: D. Spot Instances.

text:
Which of the following can be described as a global content delivery network (CDN) service?

{{c1::Amazon CloudFront.}}

Which of the following services allows customers to manage their agreements with AWS?

{{c1::AWS Artifact.}}

question: Which of the following are examples of AWS-Managed Services, where AWS is responsible for the operational and maintenance burdens of running the service? (Choose TWO)
explanation:
B. Amazon DynamoDB.
C. Amazon Elastic MapReduce.

question: Your company has a data store application that requires access to a NoSQL database. Which AWS database offering would meet this requirement?
explanation: B. Amazon DynamoDB.

question: As part of the Enterprise support plan, who is the primary point of contact for ongoing support needs?

choices.text: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) user.

explanation: D. Technical Account Manager (TAM).

text:

A. Amazon EC2
E. AWS Lambda

text:
Which AWS service can be used to privately store and manage versions of source code?

{{c1::AWS CodeCommit}}

Which AWS service should a cloud practitioner use to identify security vulnerabilities of an AWS account?

{{c1::AWS Trusted Advisor}}

question: A company wants to ensure its infrastructure is designed for fault tolerance and business continuity in the event of an environmental disruption. Which AWS infrastructure component should the company replicate across?
explanation: B. Availability Zones

text:
Which AWS service or feature is used to send both text and email messages from distributed applications?

{{c1::Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS)}}

question: Which AWS Cloud design principles can help increase reliability? (Choose two.)
explanation:
C. Testing recovery procedures
E. Automatically recovering from failure

question: A company is planning to launch an ecommerce site in a single AWS Region to a worldwide user base. Which AWS services will allow the company to reach users and provide low latency and high transfer speeds? (Choose two.)
explanation:
B. AWS Global Accelerator
D. Amazon CloudFront

question: A company wants to connect to AWS over a private, low-latency connection from its remote office. What is the recommended method to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Use VPC peering to create a connection.

explanation: D. Use AWS Direct Connect.

text:
Which AWS service can be used to retrieve compliance reports on demand?

{{c1::AWS Artifact}}

question: A company has an AWS-hosted website located behind an Application Load Balancer. The company wants to safeguard the website from SQL injection or cross-site scripting. Which AWS service should the company use?
explanation: B. AWS WAF

text:
How should a web application be deployed to ensure high availability in the AWS Cloud?

{{c1::Deploy multiple instances of the application in multiple Availability Zones.}}

question: A company is running a self-managed Oracle database directly on Amazon EC2 for its steady-state database. The company wants to reduce compute costs. Which option should the company use to maximize savings over a 3-year term?
explanation: C. EC2 Reserved Instances

question: An external auditor has requested that a company provide a list of all its IAM users, including the status of users' credentials and access keys. What it the SIMPLEST way to provide this information?

choices.text: Create an IAM user account for the auditor, granting the auditor administrator permissions.
Take a screenshot of each user's page in the AWS Management Console, then provide the screenshots to the auditor.
Download the IAM credential report, then provide the report to the auditor.
Download the AWS Trusted Advisor report, then provide the report to the auditor.

explanation: C. Download the IAM credential report, then provide the report to the auditor.

question: What are the benefits of consolidated billing for AWS Cloud services? (Choose two.)

choices.text: A minimal additional fee for use
Custom cost and usage budget creation

explanation:
A. Volume discounts
C. One bill for multiple accounts

question: A company is expecting a short-term spike in internet traffic for its application. During the traffic increase, the application cannot be interrupted. The company also needs to minimize cost and maximize flexibility. Which Amazon EC2 instance type should the company use to meet these requirements?
explanation: A. On-Demand Instances

question: A company wants to track AWS resource configuration changes for compliance reasons. Which AWS feature can be used to meet this requirement?
explanation: C. AWS Config rules

question: A company is building an application that needs to deliver images and videos globally with minimal latency. Which approach can the company use to accomplish this in a cost effective manner?

choices.text: Deliver the content through Amazon CloudFront.
Store the content on Amazon S3 and enable S3 cross-region replication.
Implement a VPN across multiple AWS Regions.
Deliver the content through AWS PrivateLink.

explanation: A. Deliver the content through Amazon CloudFront.

text:
The AWS IAM best practice for granting least privilege is to:

{{c1::apply an IAM policy only to IAM users who require it.}}

Which cloud computing benefit does AWS demonstrate with its ability to offer lower variable costs as a result of high purchase volumes?

{{c1::Economies of scale}}

question: A pharmaceutical company operates its infrastructure in a single AWS Region. The company has thousands of VPCs in a various AWS accounts that it wants to interconnect. Which AWS service or feature should the company use to help simplify management and reduce operational costs?
explanation: C. AWS Transit Gateway

question: How can AWS enable a company to control expenses as an application's usage changes unpredictably?

choices.text: AWS will refund the cost difference if a customer moves to larger servers.
The application can be built to scale up or down automatically as resources are needed
Spot instances will automatically be used if the price is lower than on-demand instances.
Amazon CloudWatch will automatically predict what resources are needed.

explanation: B. The application can be built to scale up or down automatically as resources are needed

text:
Which AWS service or feature can be used to prevent SQL injection attacks?

{{c1::AWS WAF}}
The five characteristics of cloud computing are on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and {{c1::measured service}}.
{{c1::Resource pooling}} means cloud providers serve multiple customers using a multi-tenant model.
Of AWS's three fundamental pricing principles (compute, storage, data transfer), you pay for data transfer {{c1::OUT}} of the cloud; data transfer IN is free.
AWS Regions are geographically isolated areas where AWS clusters data centers, and each region has multiple {{c1::Availability Zones}}.
When choosing an AWS Region, the four factors to consider are latency, compliance, services available, and {{c1::pricing}}.
AWS {{c1::Points of Presence (Edge Locations)}} are network locations that deliver content closer to end users, used by CloudFront and Global Accelerator.
Under the shared responsibility model, AWS is responsible for security {{c1::of the cloud}} (the infrastructure).
Under the shared responsibility model, the customer is responsible for security {{c1::in the cloud}} (their applications and data).
Amazon Route 53 is AWS's managed {{c1::DNS (Domain Name System)}} service.
In Route 53, a {{c1::CNAME}} record maps a hostname to another hostname.
In Route 53, an {{c1::Alias}} record maps a domain name directly to an AWS resource like an ELB, CloudFront, or S3.
The four Route 53 routing policies to know for the exam are simple, weighted, latency, and {{c1::failover}}.
AWS CloudFront has {{c1::216}} Points of Presence (edge locations) globally.
CloudFront improves read performance because content is {{c1::cached at the edge}}.
{{c1::S3 Transfer Acceleration}} increases transfer speed by routing uploads through the nearest AWS edge location to the target S3 bucket.
Unlike CloudFront, {{c1::AWS Global Accelerator}} does not cache content — it proxies packets at the edge to applications running in one or more AWS Regions.
{{c1::AWS Outposts}} are server racks that bring AWS infrastructure, services, and tools to run on-premises just as in the cloud.
{{c1::AWS Wavelength}} brings AWS services to the edge of 5G telecommunications networks for ultra-low latency applications.
{{c1::AWS Local Zones}} place AWS compute, storage, and database services closer to end users, acting as an extension of an AWS Region.
The AWS Well-Architected Framework has {{c1::six}} pillars.
The six pillars of the Well-Architected Framework are Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and {{c1::Sustainability}}.
The {{c1::AWS Well-Architected Tool}} is a free tool to review your architectures against the six pillars.
{{c1::AWS Right Sizing}} means matching instance types and sizes to workload performance and capacity requirements at the lowest cost.
Under the AWS Business support plan, a production system impaired case should get a response in under {{c1::4 hours}}.
Under the AWS Enterprise support plan, a business-critical system down case should get a response in under {{c1::15 minutes}}.
{{c1::AWS STS (Security Token Service)}} provides temporary, limited-privilege credentials to access AWS resources.
{{c1::Amazon Cognito}} manages identity for web and mobile application users, potentially millions, without creating IAM users.
AWS Managed Microsoft AD, AD Connector, and Simple AD are the three options under {{c1::AWS Directory Services}}.
{{c1::AD Connector}} is a directory gateway (proxy) that redirects to an on-premise Active Directory; users are managed on the on-premise AD.
{{c1::Simple AD}} is an AD-compatible managed directory on AWS that cannot be joined with an on-premise AD.
{{c1::AWS IAM Identity Center}} provides single sign-on (SSO) for AWS accounts in AWS Organizations and business cloud applications.
{{c1::Identity and Access Management (IAM)}} is a web service for securely controlling access to AWS resources.
In IAM, {{c1::Groups}} are a logical grouping of users; permissions assigned to a group are automatically inherited by its users.
In IAM, a user does not have to belong to a group and {{c1::can belong to multiple groups}}.
The five key elements of an IAM policy JSON document are Version, Statement, Action, Resource, and {{c1::Effect}}.
In an IAM policy, the Effect element is either {{c1::Allow or Deny}}.
{{c1::Inline policies}} are directly attached to a single user, group, or role (not reusable).
{{c1::Managed policies}} are reusable policies created and maintained by AWS or the customer.
The {{c1::IAM Credential Report}} lists all IAM users in the account and the status of their passwords and access keys.
The {{c1::IAM Access Advisor}} shows service permissions granted to a user and when they were last used, helping identify unused permissions.
The {{c1::IAM Policy Simulator}} lets you test the impact of IAM policies before applying them.
With MFA enabled, a user must provide their password plus {{c1::an authentication code from their MFA device}}.
The main benefit of MFA is that if a password is stolen, {{c1::the account is still not compromised}}.
A {{c1::Virtual MFA Device}} uses apps like Google Authenticator to generate a time-based one-time password.
A {{c1::U2F Security Key}} is a USB device typically used for browser-based sign-ins.
The {{c1::AWS Management Console}} is the web-based UI best suited for beginners and infrequent tasks.
{{c1::Security Groups}} act as virtual firewalls at the EC2 instance level, controlling inbound and outbound traffic.
By default, security groups deny all inbound traffic and {{c1::allow all outbound traffic}}.
EC2 {{c1::User Data}} is a script that runs only once, at the instance's first boot, to bootstrap configuration.
t2.micro is part of the AWS Free Tier, offering up to {{c1::750 hours}} per month.
{{c1::Reserved Instances}} offer up to 72% discount vs On-Demand and are recommended for steady-state, predictable workloads.
{{c1::Spot Instances}} offer up to 90% discount vs On-Demand but can be reclaimed at any time if your max price is below the current spot price.
{{c1::Dedicated Hosts}} let you book an entire physical server and are the most expensive EC2 purchasing option, useful for BYOL licensing.
{{c1::Capacity Reservations}} reserve On-Demand capacity in a specific AZ for any duration, with no time commitment and no billing discount.
{{c1::Convertible Reserved Instances}} allow changing the EC2 instance type, family, OS, scope, and tenancy, for up to 66% discount.
SSH uses port {{c1::22}}.
RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) uses port {{c1::3389}}.
{{c1::Amazon EBS}} volumes are network-attached, persistent block storage bound to a specific Availability Zone.
An EBS volume can only be mounted to {{c1::one instance at a time}}.
By default, the root EBS volume is deleted on termination, but any other attached EBS volume is {{c1::not deleted}} by default.
EBS Snapshots are stored in {{c1::Amazon S3}} and only save the blocks changed since the last snapshot (incremental).
{{c1::Amazon EFS}} is a fully managed NFS file system that can be mounted on hundreds of EC2 instances across multiple AZs.
EFS Infrequent Access (EFS-IA) offers up to {{c1::92%}} lower cost compared to EFS Standard.
{{c1::EC2 Instance Store}} provides ephemeral, high I/O storage directly attached to the instance, but data is lost when the instance stops or terminates.
An {{c1::AMI (Amazon Machine Image)}} is a template used to launch EC2 instances, containing an OS, application server, and applications.
{{c1::Amazon FSx for Lustre}} is a high-performance file system optimized for compute-heavy workloads like machine learning and HPC.
{{c1::Vertical scaling}} means increasing the capacity of a single instance, and is limited by hardware constraints.
{{c1::Horizontal scaling}} (= elasticity) means adding more instances to distribute the load.
The three types of Elastic Load Balancer are Application Load Balancer (Layer 7), Network Load Balancer (Layer 4), and {{c1::Classic Load Balancer}}.
The {{c1::Application Load Balancer (ALB)}} operates at Layer 7 and handles HTTP/HTTPS traffic.
The {{c1::Network Load Balancer (NLB)}} operates at Layer 4 and handles high-performance TCP traffic.
An {{c1::Auto Scaling Group (ASG)}} automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances to match demand and replaces unhealthy instances.
{{c1::Target Tracking Scaling}} is an ASG dynamic scaling strategy where you set a target metric value (e.g., keep average CPU at 40%).
{{c1::Predictive Scaling}} uses machine learning to predict future traffic patterns and scales proactively.
Private Docker images are stored in {{c1::Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry)}}.
ECS has two launch modes: EC2 (self-managed instances) and {{c1::Fargate}} (serverless).
{{c1::Fargate}} is a serverless compute engine for containers that works with ECS and EKS, removing the need to manage EC2 instances.
AWS Lambda's free tier includes 1,000,000 requests and {{c1::400,000 GB-seconds}} of compute time per month.
The maximum execution duration for a single AWS Lambda invocation is {{c1::15 minutes}}.
{{c1::Amazon API Gateway}} is a managed service for creating, publishing, and monitoring REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs, often paired with Lambda.
{{c1::AWS Batch}} dynamically provisions compute resources (EC2 or Spot) to efficiently run large numbers of batch computing jobs.
{{c1::Amazon Lightsail}} offers virtual servers, storage, and databases with simple, predictable pricing — a simpler alternative to EC2/RDS/ELB for beginners.
An {{c1::Internet Gateway (IGW)}} connects a VPC to the internet.
A {{c1::NAT Gateway}} allows instances in a private subnet to initiate outbound internet traffic while preventing unsolicited inbound traffic.
{{c1::Network ACLs}} operate at the subnet level and support both allow and deny rules, and are stateless.
{{c1::Security Groups}} operate at the instance level, support only allow rules, and are stateful.
A stateful firewall like a Security Group automatically allows {{c1::return traffic}}, regardless of inbound/outbound rules.
{{c1::VPC Peering}} connects two VPCs directly and requires non-overlapping CIDR ranges; it is non-transitive.
{{c1::VPC Endpoints}} allow private connectivity to AWS services without going over the public internet.
S3 and DynamoDB use a {{c1::VPC Endpoint Gateway}}; all other services use a VPC Endpoint Interface.
{{c1::Site-to-Site VPN}} connects an on-premises network to AWS over the public internet, with automatic encryption.
{{c1::AWS Direct Connect}} establishes a private, physical connection between on-premises and AWS, and takes at least a month to set up.
{{c1::AWS Transit Gateway}} allows connecting thousands of VPCs and on-premises networks through a single centralized gateway.
Amazon {{c1::Aurora}} is compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL, and is up to 5x faster than MySQL.
RDS Read Replicas use {{c1::asynchronous}} replication to improve read performance, and you can create up to 5 of them.
RDS {{c1::Multi-AZ}} provides automatic failover for high availability, with data only read/written to the main database.
{{c1::Amazon ElastiCache}} is an in-memory caching service supporting Redis and Memcached, reducing database load.
{{c1::DynamoDB}} is a fully managed, serverless NoSQL database with single-digit millisecond latency, replicated across 3 AZs.
{{c1::DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX)}} is an in-memory cache for DynamoDB offering up to 10x faster reads.
{{c1::Amazon Redshift}} is a managed data warehouse optimized for OLAP using columnar storage.
{{c1::Amazon EMR}} is a managed big data platform using Hadoop, Apache Spark, and Hive.
{{c1::Amazon Athena}} is a serverless query service that uses SQL to query data directly in S3, priced at $5 per TB scanned.
{{c1::Amazon QuickSight}} is a serverless business intelligence tool for building interactive dashboards, with pay-per-session pricing.
{{c1::Amazon Neptune}} is a fully managed graph database, great for social networks, knowledge graphs, and fraud detection.
{{c1::Amazon QLDB (Quantum Ledger Database)}} is an immutable, cryptographically verifiable ledger database for tracking financial transaction history.
The key difference between QLDB and Amazon Managed Blockchain is that QLDB has {{c1::no decentralization component}}.
{{c1::AWS Glue}} is a fully serverless, managed ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service with a Data Catalog usable by Athena, Redshift, and EMR.
{{c1::AWS DMS (Database Migration Service)}} migrates databases to AWS while keeping the source database available during migration.
S3 bucket names must be globally unique and are defined at the {{c1::region}} level.
The maximum S3 object size is {{c1::5TB}}, and uploads over 5GB must use multi-part upload.
S3 provides {{c1::99.999999999%}} (11 nines) durability of objects across multiple AZs.
S3 Standard offers {{c1::99.99%}} availability, meaning about 53 minutes of downtime per year.
{{c1::S3 Standard-IA}} offers 99.9% availability and is used for data that needs rapid access but is accessed less frequently, e.g. disaster recovery/backups.
{{c1::S3 One Zone-IA}} stores data in a single AZ, so data is lost if that AZ is destroyed.
{{c1::S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval}} offers millisecond retrieval and is great for data accessed about once a quarter.
{{c1::S3 Glacier Deep Archive}} is the cheapest S3 storage class, meant for long-term storage, with retrieval taking 12-48 hours.
{{c1::S3 Intelligent-Tiering}} automatically moves objects between access tiers based on usage, with no retrieval charges.
S3 {{c1::Versioning}} is enabled at the bucket level and protects against unintended deletes by allowing rollback to a previous version.
S3 Cross-Region Replication and Same-Region Replication both require {{c1::versioning}} to be enabled on source and destination buckets.
{{c1::S3 Object Lock}} adopts a WORM (Write Once Read Many) model, blocking object version deletion for a specified time.
AWS Snowball is recommended when data transfer would take {{c1::more than a week}} over the network.
{{c1::AWS Snowmobile}} is better than Snowball if you need to transfer more than 10 PB of data, with each unit holding up to 100 PB.
{{c1::AWS Storage Gateway}} bridges on-premises data with cloud data in S3 for hybrid storage.
{{c1::AWS Shield Standard}} protects against DDoS attacks at no additional cost for all customers.
{{c1::AWS WAF}} operates at Layer 7 and filters requests based on rules like SQL injection and XSS protection.
{{c1::AWS KMS (Key Management Service)}} lets AWS manage encryption keys on your behalf for services like EBS, S3, and RDS.
{{c1::AWS CloudHSM}} provides dedicated hardware-based encryption keys where you manage the keys entirely (not AWS).
{{c1::AWS Certificate Manager (ACM)}} lets you provision, manage, and deploy free public SSL/TLS certificates.
{{c1::AWS Secrets Manager}} securely stores and rotates confidential information like passwords and API keys, integrated with KMS.
{{c1::AWS Artifact}} provides on-demand access to AWS security and compliance reports like ISO, PCI, and SOC.
{{c1::Amazon GuardDuty}} uses machine learning and anomaly detection on CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to detect malicious activity.
{{c1::Amazon Inspector}} automatically scans EC2 instances and container images for software vulnerabilities.
{{c1::AWS Config}} records and audits the configuration and compliance of your AWS resources over time.
{{c1::Amazon Macie}} uses machine learning to discover and protect sensitive data like PII in S3.
{{c1::AWS Security Hub}} aggregates security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and other services into a central dashboard.
{{c1::Amazon Detective}} analyzes and investigates the root cause of security findings using machine learning and graphs.
{{c1::IAM Access Analyzer}} scans resource policies to find any unintended public or cross-account access.
Actions like closing the AWS account or changing the support plan can only be performed by the {{c1::root user}}.
By default, EC2 instances send CloudWatch metrics every {{c1::5 minutes}}; Detailed Monitoring reduces this to 1 minute.
{{c1::CloudWatch Alarms}} trigger notifications or automated actions (like stopping/rebooting an EC2 instance) when a metric crosses a threshold.
{{c1::AWS CloudTrail}} is enabled by default and tracks API calls made within your AWS account for auditing and governance.
If a resource is unexpectedly deleted in AWS, you should investigate {{c1::CloudTrail}} first.
CloudTrail events are stored for {{c1::90 days}}; to keep them longer, log them to S3.
{{c1::AWS X-Ray}} helps analyze and debug distributed applications by tracing requests across services.
{{c1::Amazon CodeGuru Reviewer}} uses machine learning for automated code reviews, identifying security vulnerabilities and inefficiencies.
{{c1::Amazon CodeGuru Profiler}} helps understand application runtime behavior and reduce compute costs.
The {{c1::AWS Personal Health Dashboard}} shows how AWS outages and events directly impact your specific AWS resources.
{{c1::AWS CloudFormation}} is a declarative way to define and provision AWS infrastructure as code.
The {{c1::AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK)}} lets you define infrastructure using a familiar programming language, which compiles into a CloudFormation template.
{{c1::AWS Elastic Beanstalk}} is a Platform as a Service (PaaS) for deploying applications — you're only responsible for the application code.
{{c1::AWS CodeCommit}} is a fully managed, private Git-based source control service, AWS's alternative to GitHub.
{{c1::AWS CodeBuild}} compiles source code, runs tests, and produces deployable packages, with pay-as-you-go pricing for build time.
{{c1::AWS CodeDeploy}} automates code deployment to EC2 instances and on-premises servers.
{{c1::AWS CodePipeline}} orchestrates the CI/CD steps: Code, Build, Test, Provision, Deploy.
{{c1::AWS CodeArtifact}} is a secure, scalable artifact management service for software package dependencies (Maven, npm, pip, etc.).
{{c1::AWS Cloud9}} is a cloud-based IDE accessible from a web browser, supporting real-time collaboration.
{{c1::AWS Systems Manager (SSM)}} helps manage EC2 and on-premises systems at scale, including patching automation and the Parameter Store.
{{c1::SSM Session Manager}} allows a secure shell into EC2 and on-premises servers without needing SSH keys, bastion hosts, or port 22.
{{c1::AWS OpsWorks}} is AWS's managed Chef and Puppet service, an alternative to AWS Systems Manager.
AWS Organizations lets you consolidate billing so you get {{c1::one bill}} for all accounts and share volume discounts.
{{c1::Service Control Policies (SCP)}} whitelist or blacklist IAM actions at the OU or account level, but do not apply to the master/management account.
SCPs must have an explicit {{c1::Allow}} — they don't allow anything by default.
{{c1::AWS Control Tower}} runs on top of AWS Organizations to easily set up and govern a secure, compliant multi-account environment.
{{c1::AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM)}} lets you share AWS resources you own with other accounts, avoiding duplication.
{{c1::AWS Service Catalog}} lets admins define a self-service portal of pre-approved products for users to launch.
AWS has four pricing models: pay as you go, save when you reserve, pay less by using more, and {{c1::pay less as AWS grows}}.
{{c1::AWS Compute Optimizer}} uses machine learning to recommend optimal resource configurations, lowering costs by up to 25%.
{{c1::AWS Cost Explorer}} lets you visualize costs and usage over time, and forecast usage up to 12 months ahead.
{{c1::AWS Budgets}} lets you set alerts when costs, usage, or RI utilization exceed a defined budget; the first 2 budgets are free.
Billing alarms in CloudWatch are stored in the {{c1::us-east-1}} region.
{{c1::AWS Cost Anomaly Detection}} uses machine learning to detect unusual spending patterns without needing manually defined thresholds.
The AWS Basic support plan includes access to only the {{c1::7 core}} Trusted Advisor checks.
The AWS Business and Enterprise support plans provide access to the {{c1::full set}} of Trusted Advisor checks.
Under the AWS Enterprise support plan, you get access to a designated {{c1::Technical Account Manager (TAM)}}.
Under the AWS Business support plan, a production system down case should get a response in under {{c1::1 hour}}.
{{c1::Amazon SQS}} is a fully managed queue service used to decouple applications, with a default message retention of 4 days.
SQS supports standard queues (unlimited throughput) and {{c1::FIFO queues}} (ordered processing).
{{c1::Amazon SNS}} follows a pub/sub model — publishers send one message to a topic, and all subscribers receive it.
{{c1::Amazon Kinesis}} is used for real-time big data streaming at scale.
{{c1::Amazon MQ}} is a managed message broker for traditional open protocols (MQTT, AMQP, STOMP) and runs on a dedicated machine, unlike serverless SQS/SNS.
{{c1::Amazon Rekognition}} identifies objects, people, text, and scenes in images and videos using machine learning.
{{c1::Amazon Transcribe}} converts speech to text using automatic speech recognition (ASR).
{{c1::Amazon Polly}} turns text into lifelike speech.
{{c1::Amazon Translate}} provides natural language translation between languages.
{{c1::Amazon Lex}} uses the same technology that powers Alexa, converting speech to text and recognizing intent for chatbots.
{{c1::Amazon Comprehend}} is a fully managed NLP service that extracts key phrases, entities, and sentiment from text.
{{c1::Amazon SageMaker}} is a fully managed service for developers and data scientists to build, train, and deploy ML models.
{{c1::Amazon Forecast}} uses machine learning to deliver highly accurate time-series forecasts.
{{c1::Amazon Kendra}} is a fully managed document search service powered by machine learning and natural language.
{{c1::Amazon Personalize}} is a fully managed ML service for real-time personalized product recommendations.
{{c1::Amazon Textract}} automatically extracts text, handwriting, and data from scanned documents.
{{c1::Amazon WorkSpaces}} is a managed Desktop as a Service (DaaS) solution for Windows or Linux desktops.
{{c1::Amazon AppStream 2.0}} streams desktop applications to any device through a web browser, without provisioning a full VDI.
{{c1::AWS IoT Core}} lets you connect Internet of Things devices to the AWS Cloud securely and at scale.
{{c1::AWS AppSync}} stores and syncs data in real-time across mobile and web apps using GraphQL.
{{c1::AWS Amplify}} is a set of tools for building and deploying full-stack web and mobile applications, and can leverage AppSync in the background.
{{c1::AWS Backup}} is a fully managed service to centrally automate and manage backups across AWS services.
{{c1::AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS)}} quickly recovers physical, virtual, and cloud-based servers into AWS using continuous block-level replication.
{{c1::AWS DataSync}} moves large amounts of data from on-premises to AWS, with incremental replication after the first full load.
{{c1::AWS Application Migration Service (MGN)}} is AWS's lift-and-shift solution, replacing the AWS Server Migration Service (SMS).
{{c1::AWS Migration Hub}} is a central location to track the status of your migrations to AWS.
{{c1::AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS)}} runs chaos engineering experiments to uncover hidden bugs by injecting faults into AWS workloads.
{{c1::AWS Step Functions}} builds serverless visual workflows to orchestrate Lambda functions and other AWS services.
